assumptions: The question concerns consolidated financial statements, not separate or individual financial statements.
The dividend has already been recognized as an intragroup receivable, so the fact pattern involves a recognized balance rather than a merely forecast transaction.
No facts are provided showing that the receivable is part of a net investment in a foreign operation.
A foreign-currency exposure exists only if the receivable/payable is denominated in a currency different from at least one relevant entity's functional currency.

approaches.label: General intragroup prohibition in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, hedged items generally must involve parties external to the reporting entity. Intragroup items are therefore excluded unless a specific exception applies.
approaches.practical_implication: Absent a specific exception, an intragroup dividend receivable cannot be designated as the hedged item in consolidation.

source_contributions.summary: IFRS 9 6.3.5 limits hedged items in consolidated financial statements to external-party exposures, with only stated exceptions.

approaches.label: FX risk of a recognized intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 expressly permits the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item, such as a receivable/payable, to qualify in consolidated financial statements if the resulting FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation. A designated risk component must be separately identifiable and reliably measurable.
conditions: the dividend receivable is a recognized intragroup monetary item
the relevant group entities have different functional currencies
the FX gains or losses on the item are not fully eliminated on consolidation
the designated FX risk component is separately identifiable and reliably measurable
formal hedge designation, documentation and effectiveness requirements are met
approaches.practical_implication: A recognized intragroup dividend receivable may be designated in consolidation only for its qualifying FX risk component and only if the consolidation exception is satisfied.

source_contributions.summary: IFRS 9 6.3.6 permits FX risk on intragroup monetary items in consolidated financial statements when FX differences are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
IFRS 9 6.3.7 and B6.3.8 require a designated risk component to be separately identifiable and reliably measurable.
IFRS 9 6.4.1 requires formal designation/documentation and hedge effectiveness criteria.

approaches.label: Forecast intragroup transaction cash flow hedge
approaches.reasoning: This approach applies to highly probable forecast intragroup transactions whose foreign currency risk will affect consolidated profit or loss. A recognized dividend receivable is not a forecast transaction.
approaches.practical_implication: An already recognized intragroup dividend receivable should not be analysed under the forecast-transaction exception.

source_contributions.summary: IFRS 9 6.3.3, 6.3.6 and B6.3.5–B6.3.6 allow certain highly probable forecast intragroup transactions only when the FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge framework
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 applies only to hedges of the foreign currency risk of a net investment in a foreign operation. An ordinary intragroup dividend receivable is not, on the stated facts, analysed as a net investment hedge item.
approaches.practical_implication: Unless the balance is separately supportable as part of a net investment, IFRIC 16 is not the governing hedge model.

source_contributions.summary: IFRIC 16 confines its scope to hedges of net investments in foreign operations and limits the hedged risk to the relevant foreign currency exposure of that net investment.
IFRS 9 6.5.2 identifies a hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation as a separate hedge type.
